title: Transferrin Saturation Calculator
tagline: Calculate transferrin saturation percentage to assess iron status and diagnose iron deficiency anemia or iron overload conditions.
intro: Enter serum iron and total iron binding capacity (TIBC) to instantly compute transferrin saturation and interpret iron status.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for both serum iron and TIBC.
error.tibcZero: TIBC must be greater than zero.

interpretation.high: High — possible iron overload or hemochromatosis (>50%)

about.heading: About the Transferrin Saturation Calculator
about.body:
Transferrin saturation is a key laboratory measure used to evaluate how much of the body's iron-transport protein — transferrin — is currently loaded with iron. Transferrin is synthesized by the liver and acts as the primary carrier of iron in the bloodstream, shuttling it from sites of absorption in the intestine and recycling in the spleen to tissues that need it, especially the bone marrow for red blood cell production. The transferrin saturation percentage tells clinicians what fraction of total available binding sites on transferrin are occupied by iron at any given time.

The calculation itself is straightforward: divide the serum iron concentration (µg/dL) by the total iron binding capacity (TIBC, also in µg/dL) and multiply by 100 to express the result as a percentage. TIBC is an indirect measure of circulating transferrin; it represents the maximum amount of iron that the blood could carry if every transferrin molecule were fully saturated. In practice, only a portion of those sites are occupied, which is what the saturation percentage captures.

Understanding reference ranges is essential for correct interpretation. In healthy adults, transferrin saturation typically falls between 20% and 50%. Values below 20% suggest that iron stores are insufficient — the body has depleted its reserves and is now drawing on circulating iron, a hallmark of iron deficiency anemia. Symptoms at this stage often include fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, and reduced exercise tolerance. By contrast, transferrin saturation persistently above 50% raises concern for iron overload. This pattern is characteristic of hereditary hemochromatosis, a common genetic condition in which the intestine absorbs excessive dietary iron, progressively damaging the liver, heart, joints, and endocrine organs.

Clinicians rarely interpret transferrin saturation in isolation. It is most informative when evaluated alongside serum ferritin (a marker of iron storage), complete blood count, and sometimes a reticulocyte hemoglobin assay. For example, a low transferrin saturation combined with a low ferritin and microcytic anemia strongly supports iron deficiency, whereas a high saturation with an elevated ferritin and normal or high hemoglobin points toward overload. The combination of a fasting transferrin saturation above 45% and an elevated ferritin is the standard screening test for hereditary hemochromatosis, prompting genetic testing for HFE mutations.

This calculator provides educational estimates based on the values you enter. Results should always be interpreted in the context of a full clinical assessment. Factors such as recent meals (iron is best measured fasting), inflammation (acute-phase reactions lower serum iron and raise ferritin independently of iron stores), pregnancy (increases TIBC), and oral contraceptive use can all influence individual values. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional before acting on laboratory results.

examples.heading: Transferrin saturation examples
examples.intro: The table below shows how different serum iron and TIBC combinations map to transferrin saturation and clinical interpretation.

items.note: Normal range — adequate iron stores, no evidence of deficiency or overload.
Low saturation — consistent with iron deficiency anemia; TIBC is elevated as the body tries to absorb more iron.
High saturation — suggests iron overload; warrants further evaluation for hemochromatosis.
Mid-normal range — typically observed in healthy individuals with adequate iron intake.

howto.heading: How to use the Transferrin Saturation Calculator
steps: Obtain your serum iron and TIBC values from a recent iron panel blood test (ideally drawn fasting).
Enter the serum iron concentration in µg/dL into the Serum Iron field.
Enter the TIBC value in µg/dL into the TIBC field.
Click Calculate Transferrin Saturation to see the percentage and interpretation.
Review the result alongside your other iron studies and discuss with your healthcare provider.

items.q: What is a normal transferrin saturation?
items.a: A normal transferrin saturation is generally 20–50% in adults. Values below 20% may indicate iron deficiency, while values above 50% may suggest iron overload conditions such as hemochromatosis. Reference ranges can vary slightly between laboratories.

items.q: What does low transferrin saturation mean?
items.a: A low transferrin saturation (below 20%) suggests that less iron than normal is bound to transferrin, often indicating iron deficiency anemia. In this state the body typically compensates by increasing TIBC to capture any available iron from the diet.

items.q: What does high transferrin saturation indicate?
items.a: A high transferrin saturation (above 50%) means that transferrin is heavily loaded with iron, raising concern for iron overload. Hereditary hemochromatosis is the most common cause, but it can also occur with excessive iron supplementation, repeated blood transfusions, or certain liver diseases.

items.q: Why should iron studies be drawn fasting?
items.a: Serum iron concentrations fluctuate significantly after meals, so testing is ideally performed after an overnight fast. A non-fasting sample can give falsely elevated serum iron, leading to a spuriously high transferrin saturation and potentially missing a deficiency.

items.q: How is transferrin saturation different from serum ferritin?
items.a: Serum ferritin reflects iron storage within cells, while transferrin saturation reflects the fraction of circulating iron-transport protein carrying iron. Both tests together provide a more complete picture: low ferritin plus low saturation strongly suggests true iron deficiency, whereas high ferritin plus high saturation points toward overload.

items.q: Is this calculator a substitute for medical advice?
items.a: No. This tool is intended for educational purposes only. Laboratory values must be interpreted in the context of your full clinical history, symptoms, and other test results. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for diagnosis and treatment.
